items.q: Why might managed retreat be more sustainable than building a sea wall?
items.correct: Sea walls are expensive, deflect erosion elsewhere and reflect wave energy worsening beach loss; managed retreat works with natural processes, creates habitat and is cheaper long-term.
items.misconception: Sea walls always solve coastal erosion problems permanently.
items.random_wrong: Managed retreat means letting all coastal towns disappear into the sea.
items.why: Sea walls protect one stretch but cause terminal scour and sediment starvation downdrift. Managed retreat selectively allows the coast to adjust — controversial socially but ecologically sound.

items.q: Why do tropical cyclones only form over warm tropical oceans?
items.correct: They need sea surface temperatures above ~27 degrees C to a depth of 50m to provide energy through evaporation, plus enough Coriolis effect (away from the equator) to spin.
items.misconception: Hurricanes form wherever there are strong winds over the sea.
items.random_wrong: Hurricanes form when two cold fronts collide above the ocean.
items.why: Wind doesn't create hurricanes — warm ocean does. They draw energy from latent heat of evaporating seawater; that's why they weaken rapidly over land or cool water.

items.q: What is the ITCZ and why does it bring rain?
items.correct: The Intertropical Convergence Zone is where trade winds from both hemispheres converge near the equator, forcing air to rise, cool and produce heavy convectional rainfall.
items.misconception: The ITCZ is a fixed band of permanent storms at the equator.
items.random_wrong: The ITCZ is the boundary between the Atlantic and Pacific oceans.
items.why: The ITCZ shifts seasonally north and south with the sun, driving monsoon rains. Students treat it as static — its movement explains why tropical regions have wet/dry seasons.

items.q: Managed realignment at Medmerry created how many hectares of habitat?
items.why: Correct answer: 183. “83” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: £2 million. “£0.2 million” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Exact answer: 1.5. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
